title: ASIC probes DroneShield chief’s share sale
full_text:
The financial regulator has launched an investigation into share sales by former DroneShield chief executive Oleg Vornik, outgoing chairman Peter James and another director late last year.
ASIC has asked DroneShield to provide it with “reasonable assistance” with its investigation after Mr Vornik, Mr James and director Jethro Marks sold a total $70m worth of shares in the counter-drone company in November as soon as the shares were granted.
ASIC is also looking into disclosures made around the time, including a contract win that had previously been disclosed.
DroneShield says the repeat announcement was accidental.
DroneShield shares plunged as low as $2.95 on Tuesday. The the stock recovered some ground to close at $3.18, down 9.9 per cent for the day.
The impact of the ASIC news is another whack for a once-hot stock, which has yet to find significant institutional backing.
From a 52-week high of $6.71, shares in DroneShield have fallen more than 53 per cent.
Mr Vornik and Mr James pocketed $63m between them in the November share sales, immediately raising investor questions. Mr Vornik sold his entire stake in the company he had led to the ASX 200.
Prompting immediate backlash from investors and the wider business community, the company said it had grown faster than its governance could keep up, and that it had established mandatory minimum shareholding rules for its executive team.
The CEO must now hold stock worth 200 per cent of their annual income and directors are required to hold stock equivalent in value to their base rate.
Mr Vornik quit in April. Chairman Peter James will step down at the end of the annual general meeting on May 29, handing over to Hamish McLennan, also chairman of REA. DroneShield chief product officer Angus Bean is now CEO.
DroneShield told investors in a statement that it had received a notice from ASIC requiring it to provide reasonable assistance in connection with an investigation under the Corporations Act.
“DroneShield will co-operate fully with the investigation regarding announcements and information provided to the Australian Securities Exchange between 1 and 20 November 2025, and trading in DroneShield shares between 6 and 12 November 2025 (inclusive),” it said.
“It is not clear what action, if any, may result from ASIC’s investigation.” In February, at the announcement of its half-year results, a “bitter” Mr Vornik told The Australian he felt the backlash as a result of the shares sales was “a bit of tall-poppy syndrome”. Mr Vornik earlier this year said half of the money he made from the share sale was taxed, and that the rest had been used to pay for a renovation blowout and to secure his financial future.
He has also said it was just reward, as a Russian-born individual supplying defence technology to Ukraine, he had put himself in the way of considerable harm.
In its first-quarter results announced in August, DroneShield reported a 121 per cent rise on revenue, quarter-on-quarter to $74.1m, a 360 per cent rise in customer cash receipts to $77.4m and a 205 per cent lift in Saas revenues, as more global operators buy in to its AI-powered counter-drone tech.
DroneShield’s DroneGun can jam enemy drones and tech from distance out in the field. Mr Vornik was contacted.
summary: ASIC is investigating share sales by former DroneShield executives, including Oleg Vornik, following a $70 million sale that raised investor concerns.
author: Joseph Carbone
heading: Regulation
key_points: ASIC has requested assistance from DroneShield regarding share sales by executives.
The share price of DroneShield has fallen over 53% from its 52-week high.
New mandatory shareholding rules have been established for executives following the backlash.
relevance: This situation highlights the importance of corporate governance and regulatory compliance, which are critical areas of concern for actuaries and financial services professionals.
